text: Informed consent was obtained and the patient was brought to the operating room.
General anesthetic was administered and the patient was intubated with a dual lumen endotracheal tube.
He was positioned supine with a bump to elevate the right side of the body.
A five millimeter incision was made in the right anterior chest at the sixth intercostal space.
Under direct visualization, two additional five millimeter ports were placed in the mid axillary line.
Upon entry, the mass was clearly evident in the mediastinum bulging into the right chest with mammary vessels anterior to the mass and the phrenic nerve posterior to the mass.
Key structures including the azagus vein, the superior intercostal vein off of the azagus vein, intercostal veins, superior vena cava,
phrenic nerve, and mammary vessels were identified.
The medial aspect of the right lung was draped over the mass, so a plane was dissected between the visceral pleura and the pleura overlying the mass.
Hook cautery was used for the anterior dissection between the mass and the sternum.
The dissection was carried over to the left pleural space.
At this point, it was noted that the mass was fixed superiorly, and a fourth port was placed in the midaxillary line to facilitate dissection.
The mass was dissected off of the innominate vein using a combination of the ligature and blunt dissection with the endokitner.
The mass extended well into the neck and significant traction was required to successfully mobilize it off of the innominate vein and the aorta.
Attention was then turned to the portion of the mass that was adherent to the pericardium.
The endokitner was similarly used to carefully dissect the mass off of the pericardium.
After the mass was freed cranially and caudally, it could be retracted posteriorly in order to mobilize the left lateral aspect of the mass off of the pleura of the left lung.
Because the left phrenic nerve could not be identified from the right chest, an additional trochar was placed in the left chest at the fifth intercostal space in the midaxillary line.
This allowed visualization of the left phrenic nerve to verify that it remained intact throughout its course.
Finally, the mass was mobilized into the right chest and out of the mediastinum.
The mediastinum was hemostatic and the pericardium appeared intact.
The middle port site in the right midaxillary line was enlarged to allow for exteriorization of the mass in a 15mm endocutch bag.
The right chest was irrigated and a 19 French Blake drain was placed through the inferior most site into the mediastinal space.
